query: In what year was the tallest self-supporting tower completed?When ready, output the final answer enclosed in <answer> and </answer> tags. Do not generate any content after the </answer> tag.
query_analysis:
Summary of the Query:
The query asks for the year in which the tallest self-supporting tower was completed.

Necessary Skills and Tools:
- Research Skills: To gather information about the tallest self-supporting towers and their completion years.
- Historical Knowledge: To understand the context of tower construction and significant milestones.
- Analytical Skills: To compare and select the tallest self-supporting tower from the available data.

Tools:
1. **Ground_Google_Search_Tool**: This tool can help find the tallest self-supporting tower and its completion year through web searches, providing real-time information and citations.
2. **Wikipedia_RAG_Search_Tool**: This tool can be used to find detailed information about famous towers and their construction dates from reliable sources like Wikipedia.

Explanation:
- **Research Skills**: Essential for understanding the context and identifying the correct information sources.
- **Historical Knowledge**: Important to recognize the significance of self-supporting towers and the historical context of their construction.
- **Analytical Skills**: Needed to compare and verify the completion years of the tallest self-supporting towers.

Additional Considerations:
- Verify the completion year from multiple sources to ensure accuracy.
- Consider the possibility of updates in construction records and historical data.

result: The tallest self-supporting tower, the Tokyo Skytree, was completed in 2012.[1](https://www.tickets-tokyo.com/tokyo-skytree-tower/history/), [2](https://www.worldconstructionnetwork.com/projects/tokyo-sky-tree/), [3](https://simple.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree), [4](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree), [5](https://thisdayintechhistory.com/02/29/tokyo-skytree-completed/), [6](https://tvradioschedules.fandom.com/wiki/List_of_tallest_towers), [7](https://kids.kiddle.co/List_of_tallest_towers) It officially opened to the public on May 22, 2012.[1](https://www.tickets-tokyo.com/tokyo-skytree-tower/history/), [3](https://simple.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree), [4](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree) Standing at 634 meters (2,080 feet) tall, the Tokyo Skytree is located in Tokyo, Japan.[1](https://www.tickets-tokyo.com/tokyo-skytree-tower/history/), [2](https://www.worldconstructionnetwork.com/projects/tokyo-sky-tree/), [3](https://simple.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree), [4](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree), [6](https://tvradioschedules.fandom.com/wiki/List_of_tallest_towers), [7](https://kids.kiddle.co/List_of_tallest_towers) Its primary functions include digital broadcasting and providing observation decks.[1](https://www.tickets-tokyo.com/tokyo-skytree-tower/history/), [2](https://www.worldconstructionnetwork.com/projects/tokyo-sky-tree/), [3](https://simple.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree), [6](https://tvradioschedules.fandom.com/wiki/List_of_tallest_towers), [7](https://kids.kiddle.co/List_of_tallest_towers)
